--- SOURCE 1: Ethics, Animal Welfare, and Artificial Intelligence in Livestock ---
URL: https://www.mdpi.com/2624-7402/7/7/202

SUMMARY:
by TC Santana · 2025 — As noted by [49], six core categories of ethical risk have been identified: fairness, transparency, accountability, sustainability, privacy, and robustness. The

All articles published by MDPI are made immediately available worldwide under an open access license. No special permission is required to reuse all or part of the article published by MDPI, including figures and tables. For articles published under an open access Creative Common CC BY license, any part of the article may be reused without permission provided that the original article is clearly cited. For more information, please refer to [https://www.mdpi.com/openaccess](https://www.mdpi.com/openaccess).

Feature papers represent the most advanced research with significant potential for high impact in the field. A Feature Paper should be a substantial original Article that involves several techniques or approaches, provides an outlook for future research directions and describes possible research applications.

Feature papers are submitted upon individual invitation or recommendation by the scientific editors and must receive positive feedback from the reviewers.

Editor’s Choice articles are based on recommendations by the scientific editors of MDPI journals from around the world. Editors select a small number of articles recently published in the journal that they believe will be particularly interesting to readers, or important in the respective research area. The aim is to provide a snapshot of some of the most exciting work published in the various research areas of the journal.

[Review Reports](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-7402/7/7/202/review_report)[Versions Notes](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-7402/7/7/202/notes)

Abstract
--------

This study presents a bibliometric review aimed at mapping and analyzing the scientific literature related to the ethical implications of artificial intelligence (AI) in livestock farming, which is a rapidly emerging yet still underexplored field in international research. Based on the Scopus database, 151 documents published between 2015 and 2025 were identified and analyzed using the VOSviewer version 1.6.20 and Biblioshiny for Bibliometrix (RStudio version 2023.12.1) tools. The results show a significant increase in publications from 2021 onwards, reflecting the growing maturity of discussions around the integration of digital technologies in the agricultural sector. Keyword co-occurrence and bibliographic coupling analyses revealed the formation of four main thematic clusters, covering technical applications in precision livestock farming as well as reflections on governance, animal welfare, and algorithmic justice. The most influential authors, high-impact journals, and leading countries in the field were also identified. As a key contribution, this study highlights the lack of robust ethical guidelines and proposes future research directions for the development of regulatory frameworks, codes of conduct, and interdisciplinary approaches. The findings underscore the importance of aligning technological innovation with ethical responsibility and social inclusion in the transition to digital livestock farming.

Keywords: 

[artificial intelligence](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=artificial+intelligence); [digital livestock farming](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=digital+livestock+farming); [applied ethics](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=applied+ethics); [animal welfare](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=animal+welfare); [technological governance](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=technological+governance); [agriculture 4.0](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=agriculture+4.0); [bibliometrics](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=bibliometrics)

1. Introduction
---------------

Ethics in artificial intelligence (AI) is a topic of great relevance and complexity in the contemporary era. With the increasingly rapid advancement of this technology, there is an urgent need to explore the ethical implications associated with its development and application [[1](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-7402/7/7/202#B1-agriengineering-07-00202)]. # Why Ethical Challenges and Technological Advances in Applying Machine Learning to Animal Welfare Demand Urgent Attention

## Navigating Privacy, Consent, and Innovation for Responsible AI-Driven Animal Health and Care Solutions

![Shailendra Kumar](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:64:64/0*8MjIclNVcyyVD31a.jpg)

--

Share

## How can we responsibly apply machine learning to improve animal welfare while addressing ethical challenges?

When I first encountered the idea of using machine learning (ML) to improve animal welfare, I was fascinated by the possibilities. Could AI really help us understand animals better, detect their distress early, or even communicate with them? The answer is yes — but only if we carefully navigate the ethical challenges that come with these powerful technologies. Applying ML to animal welfare isn’t just about innovation; it’s about respecting the privacy, consent, and dignity of animals while harnessing technology to enhance their health and care.

I remember vividly the moment I saw a facial expression analysis system in action on a farm. The AI could detect subtle signs of discomfort in cows that humans might miss. It was a breakthrough, but it also raised questions: Are we invading the animals’ privacy? How do we ensure their consent, or even…

enriched_points.expanded_content: Utilizing technology to monitor animals raises concerns about privacy, as constant surveillance may intrude on their natural behaviors and personal space, impacting their welfare.
The absence of robust ethical frameworks for deploying animal welfare technologies can lead to inconsistent practices, risking animal dignity and welfare in the name of innovation.
Technological biases may result in inaccurate assessments of animal welfare, potentially leading to misinterpretations of their needs and affecting their treatment and care.
